title: REGIONAL STANDARD FOR DOOGH (Near East) 1 CXS 332R-2018 Adopted in 2018.

sections.section: REGIONAL STANDARD FOR DOOGH (Near East) 1 CXS 332R-2018 Adopted in 2018.
sections.summary: Document title/heading only (Regional Standard for Doogh, Near East, CXS 332R‑2018 adopted 2018) and contains no substantive provisions in this entry.
Standard applies to doogh for direct consumption or further processing, using the definitions in Section 2, and should be read in conjunction with the Standard for Fermented Milks (CXS 243-2003).

Defines doogh as a fermented-milk-based drink produced by mixing yoghurt or milk with potable water (and optionally salt) or by fermenting diluted milk, specifies typical yogurt starter cultures (Streptococcus thermophilus and Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus) and allows other microorganisms or heat treatment (which removes the viable-microorganism requirement) with product variants noted (plain/flavoured, carbonated/uncarbonated, heat treated/un-heat treated, powdered).

Sets essential composition and quality factors for doogh including permitted raw materials (yoghurt/milk and potable water), permitted ingredients (starter cultures, other microbes including probiotics, salt, natural flavourings, nutraceuticals, dairy ingredients), a prohibition on replacing milk fat/protein with non-dairy sources, and compositional and microbiological criteria (see Section 3.3) valid to the date of minimum durability.
Specifies raw materials for doogh as yoghurt (which must conform to the Standard for Fermented Milks) or milk, and potable water for dilution, reconstitution or recombination.

Lists permitted ingredients for doogh: harmless starter cultures (typical doogh starters), other harmless/beneficial microorganisms including probiotics, sodium chloride per CXS 150-1985, natural flavouring particles, nutraceuticals (e.g., dietary fibres), and dairy ingredients/products (milk proteins, powders, milk fat, buttermilk, whey) referencing relevant Codex standards.

Specifies compositional and microbiological limits for doogh: pH max 4.5; titratable acidity ≥ 0.3% lactic acid; MSNF ≥ 3.0%; milk protein ≥ 1.08% (m/m); sum of starter culture organisms ≥ 10^7 cfu/g and each labelled strain ≥ 10^7 cfu/g, with these microbiological criteria applicable up to the declared date of minimum durability under labeled storage conditions.
Section heading for food additives only; no substantive content provided in this extract.
Section heading for food additives only (duplicate); no substantive content provided in this extract.

Requires that milk used in products covered by this Standard comply with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs.

Recommends that preparation and handling of these products follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.
Requires doogh be packed in containers that preserve its hygienic and other qualities and that fermented doogh be stored under appropriate conditions, e.g., refrigerated.

Heading noting that, beyond the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206-1999), additional specific labelling provisions for doogh apply.

Specifies doogh labelling: the product name must be 'Doogh', may include descriptors 'Carbonated/Uncarbonated' and 'Heat treated/Un-heat treated' (with 'Fermenting' or 'Injecting' indicating carbonation source), 'Flavoured Doogh' if flavourings added, 'Probiotic' may be used when probiotic microorganisms are present, and powdered forms must be labelled as 'Doogh Powder' or 'Dried Doogh'.

sections.section: 8.1.5 For doogh powder, the name 'Doogh Powder' or 'Dried Doogh' shall be inserted on the label.
sections.summary: For powdered doogh, the label must state either 'Doogh Powder' or 'Dried Doogh'.
